did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier to distinguish between melanoma and nevus skin lesions. The method involved image pre-processing, segmentation using multi-thresholding, and feature extraction (18 features including color and GLCM textural features) which were fed into the ANN to obtain classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving image pre-processing, segmentation using multi-thresholding, feature extraction (18 features), and classification using a pre-trained Artificial Neural Network (ANN) on the PH² dataset. It reports specific results (100% accuracy) and compares them with existing methods, which characterizes it as a research paper. Although published in conference proceedings (ICOSEC 2020), it contains original results rather than being a review or survey.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions in Digital Images for the Diagnosis of Skin Cancer
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Smart Electronics and Communication (ICOSEC 2020)
brief_summary: This study proposes an image processing technique to classify skin lesions as melanoma or nevus for early skin cancer diagnosis. The methodology involves pre-processing dermoscopic images from the PH² dataset, segmenting the lesion using multi-thresholding, and extracting 18 color and GLCM textural features. These features are fed into a pre-trained Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier to distinguish between the two classes. The system was evaluated on 166 selected images from the PH² dataset, with 144 images used for training and 20 for testing. The proposed method achieved a classification accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of 100% on the test set. Comparative analysis indicates that this approach outperforms several existing methods in terms of accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes used in the classification task. The study used 34 melanoma images from the PH² dataset for training and included melanoma images in the test set (10 true positives). The paper also mentions melanoma survival rates and characteristics in the introduction.

One of the two primary classes used in the classification task. The study used 112 nevus images from the PH² dataset for training and included nevus images in the test set (10 true negatives). The paper refers to the classification goal as distinguishing between melanoma and nevus (or cancerous vs. non-cancerous).

Mentioned in the description of the PH² dataset contents ('this data set consists of melanoma, nevus and dysplastic nevus images'), but not explicitly stated as a distinct class in the binary classification experiment performed by the authors.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or nevus (cancerous vs. non-cancerous).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify skin lesion image into melanoma or nevus' using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). The target is discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevus). The model achieved 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the test set.

datasets_used.name: PH² dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset obtained from the dermatology service of Hospital Pedro Hispano. It contains images of melanoma, nevus, and dysplastic nevus with clinically diagnosed results. The images have a resolution of 768x560 pixels and include colors such as white, red, light brown, dark brown, blue grey, and black, along with legends for pigment network, dots/globules, streaks, regression areas, and blue-whitish veil.

use.training: 146 images (34 melanoma and 112 nevus) were used to train the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier.
use.testing: 20 images were used to test the classification results.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate classification performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity by counting true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
To compare the proposed system's accuracy (100%) against existing methods reported in literature (e.g., K-NN, SVM, MLP classifiers) using accuracy as the primary metric.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity derived from a confusion matrix, and compared qualitatively with existing methods.

PH² dataset.notes: The proposed system was evaluated on 166 selected images from the PH² dataset (34 melanoma and 112 nevus for training; 20 images for testing). The comparative analysis in Table 1 lists results from other studies using different datasets (ISIC, Dermis) and classifiers (K-NN, MLP, SVM). The proposed system outperformed these methods in terms of accuracy on the PH² dataset.

architecture_used.input_size: 18 features (12 color features and 6 GLCM textural features)

architecture_details.layers: 3 layers (1 Input layer, 1 Hidden layer, 1 Output layer)
architecture_details.hidden_neurons: Not explicitly specified, but total neurons are 21
architecture_details.output_neurons: Not explicitly specified, but binary classification (Melanoma vs Nevus)

training_details.hyperparameters: Gaussian blurring parameter = 6 (for pre-processing, not network training)

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian blurring (smoothing) with a blurring parameter of 6 to remove noise
Hair removal (artifact removal) using morphological bottom hat filter followed by a closing operation
Multi-thresholding segmentation using Otsu's method to separate lesion from background
conclusions: The proposed image processing technique using multi-thresholding segmentation and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier successfully distinguishes between melanoma and nevus skin lesions.
The system achieved 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on a test set of 20 images from the PH² dataset.
The method utilizes a 18-feature vector comprising 12 color features and 6 GLCM textural features extracted from the segmented lesion region.
The proposed system outperformed existing methods reported in the literature (such as those using K-NN, SVM, and MLP classifiers) in terms of classification accuracy.

The multi-thresholding approach effectively segments the lesion region from the background without processing unwanted background regions, contributing to accurate feature extraction and classification.
The system is described as less time-consuming compared to other methodologies.
The study acknowledges that the performance depends heavily on the quality of the primary stages, specifically blurring and segmentation.
important_remarks: The study reports 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on a very small test set of only 20 images, which is insufficient to establish robust generalizability.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with 112 nevus images used for training compared to only 34 melanoma images, potentially biasing the classifier towards the majority class.

The comparative analysis in Table 1 is methodologically flawed because it compares the proposed method (using the PH² dataset) against other studies that used different datasets (ISIC, Dermis) and different feature sets, making direct performance comparisons invalid.
The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests for the reported metrics, limiting the statistical rigor of the claims.

The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) architecture is extremely simple (21 neurons, 3 layers) and lacks details on training hyperparameters (optimizer, loss function, learning rate, epochs), making reproducibility difficult.

The term 'pre-trained ANN' is used in the abstract and conclusion, but the methodology section describes a standard feedforward network trained on extracted features without specifying what it was pre-trained on or if it was trained from scratch, creating ambiguity.

The study relies on a single source of images (PH² dataset) from one hospital, raising concerns about external validation and potential bias related to image acquisition equipment or patient demographics.
The segmentation method uses multi-thresholding with Otsu's method, which may be sensitive to image quality and lighting conditions, yet no robustness analysis or failure cases are reported.
